title: The Missile and the Chokepoint
subhead: Turkey unveiled an ICBM and held the lira through Hormuz — but the war on its border is converting temporary indispensability into permanent exposure faster than Ankara is converting it into institutional power.

sections.heading: Yildirimhan and the Illusion of Autonomy
sections.body:
Turkey unveiled its first intercontinental ballistic missile at SAHA 2026 in Istanbul on May 5-9. The Yildirimhan — 'Thunderbolt Khan' — is a liquid-fueled system with a 6,000 km range, Mach 9–25 speed, nitrogen tetroxide propellant, and a 3,000 kg payload. Defense Minister Yaşar Güler presented it as the crown jewel of Turkey's indigenous defense industrial ecosystem. A propellant breakthrough was separately touted on May 8 as enabling longer burn times and greater range precision.

The structural problem is that an ICBM without an integrated early-warning and command-and-control architecture is a prestige symbol, not a deterrent. Turkey cannot activate its Russian S-400s without triggering CAATSA sanctions, and it lacks indigenous ballistic missile defense capable of intercepting incoming Iranian SRBMs — let alone ICBM-class threats. NATO interceptors, not Turkish systems, shot down the four Iranian missiles that entered Turkish airspace in March and April. The Yildirimhan signals intent, but it does not close the air defense gap that makes Turkey a dependent power in the most consequential security theater on its border.

The decisive move is not more missiles — it is air defense sovereignty. Turkey needs either a path back to Patriot/FDI acquisition without S-400 preconditions, or a crash program to field the SIPER system at scale. Without missile defense, the ICBM is a one-way capability in a two-way threat environment. The trap: Ankara bets on prestige platforms while its actual vulnerability — the inability to defend its own airspace independently — remains structurally unaddressed.
sections.whyItMatters: A country that can strike at 6,000 km but cannot intercept missiles crossing its own airspace has the worst of both worlds: escalation capability without escalation dominance. That is not autonomy — it is exposure with a bigger stick.

sections.heading: Neutrality's Expiration Date on the Iran Border
sections.body:
Turkey's neutrality in the Iran war — modeled on its World War II balancing act — is under structural strain. Four Iranian ballistic missiles entered Turkish airspace between March and April, targeting NATO infrastructure at Kürecik and Incirlik. All were intercepted by NATO assets, not Turkish systems. Ankara declined to invoke Article 5. Turkey has refused the U.S. and Israel use of its airspace for strikes on Iran, and has positioned itself as a message-passer between Washington and Tehran alongside Pakistan, Oman, and Egypt.

The Foreign Affairs analysis makes the core structural point plain: the war's outcomes — Iranian state fragmentation, an emboldened Israel, a hardened IRGC — all run against Turkish interests regardless of which side wins. A shattered Iran sends refugees and revives Kurdish separatism; a dominant Israel reshapes the Eastern Mediterranean and Syria against Turkish positioning. Neutrality does not insulate Turkey from either outcome — it merely ensures Ankara has no leverage over which one materializes.

Turkey's mediation has been real but marginal. Reuters confirmed Ankara passed messages between Washington and Tehran. Secretary of State Rubio told Fidan that strikes on Turkish territory were 'unacceptable.' Iran's foreign ministry denied targeting Turkey and agreed to a joint deconfliction mechanism. But the Stimson Center assessment is blunt: Turkey's credibility as mediator is limited by Israeli distrust, vulnerability to the very missiles it is mediating about, and domestic political incentives for anti-U.S. rhetoric that erode Washington's confidence.

The decisive move is to convert deconfliction into leverage. The technical communications channel with Israel in Syria, brokered by Washington, is a start — but it is explicitly limited to accident-avoidance. Turkey's real play is to make its Kürecik radar and Incirlik hosting indispensable enough that any postwar settlement requires Turkish buy-in. The trap: if the war ends through a U.S.-Iran deal that bypasses Turkey entirely, Ankara's three months of mediation and four intercepted missiles yield zero institutional dividends.
sections.whyItMatters: WWII neutrality worked because the war ended with Turkey's alliance value intact and the postwar order was built around NATO membership. The Iran war's post-conflict order is consolidating now — through PESCO, through Gulf security recalibrations, through Israeli operational dominance in Syria. Every day Turkey remains a spectator is a day the new architecture gets built without Turkish input.

sections.heading: PESCO Exclusion: The Structural Amputation
sections.body:
Defense Minister Güler delivered Ankara's clearest warning yet at the NATO 74th anniversary conference on April 9: excluding Turkey from EU defense initiatives would harm European security more than a U.S. troop drawdown. Turkey applied to join PESCO's Military Mobility project. The application is blocked by Greek and Cypriot vetoes operating on the unanimity rule. Turkey will assume command of NATO's Allied Reaction Force from 2028–2030.

PESCO exclusion is not a diplomatic inconvenience — it is a structural amputation. Every defense procurement decision, every capability target, every operational concept that consolidates without Turkish input makes decisions that directly affect Turkish security interests. Greece and Cyprus understand this perfectly: for them, EU defense cooperation is partially designed to balance Turkey in the Eastern Mediterranean. The EU is building a defense architecture that treats Turkey as the threat it must defend against, not the ally it must defend with.

The Carnegie Europe analysis (May 8) confirms the fundamental impasse: Europe's need for Turkish defense capacity collides with its political inability to grant Turkey institutional parity. Eastern flank states — Poland, Romania, the Baltics — recognize Turkey's value. France, Germany, and the Brussels bureaucracy treat Turkey as a geopolitical actor to be managed, not a strategic partner to be integrated. The SWP Berlin assessment adds that Turkey has a 'strategically significant, albeit politically contentious' role in both Ukrainian and Black Sea security — yet no institutional channel translates that role into EU decision-making.

Defense News reports that Turkey's defense industry is compelling European capitals toward cooperation — drones, armored vehicles, naval platforms, and munitions at scale. But industrial demand does not override political vetoes. The decisive move is bilateral: Turkey should pursue defense agreements with individual EU states and the Eastern flank outside PESCO frameworks, creating facts on the ground that make PESCO exclusion functionally irrelevant. The trap: waiting for a unanimous PESCO vote is a forever trap designed by adversaries.
sections.whyItMatters: A defense architecture that excludes the alliance's second-largest military and its only Middle Eastern power-projection capability is not a defense architecture — it is a political statement. The question is whether Turkey builds alternative integration paths or accepts permanent marginalization in the name of Greek-Cypriot veto rights.

sections.heading: Hormuz Shock and the Permanent Energy Deficit
sections.body:
The World Bank's Commodity Markets Outlook (May 7) confirms the Strait of Hormuz remains 'practically closed,' triggering what it calls the largest oil market shock in history. Brent crude has surpassed $100 per barrel. UNCTAD projects global trade growth collapsing from 4.7% in 2025 to 1.5–2.5% in 2026. Turkey's energy minister has said Middle East oil dependence is 'manageable' at around 10% of supply — but TEPAV warned in March that every $10 increase in oil prices widens Turkey's current account deficit by at least $2.5 billion and adds roughly 1 percentage point to inflation. S&P hiked its Turkish inflation outlook in March specifically over energy fallout from the war.

Turkey's vulnerability isn't temporary — it's structural. A country that imports roughly 90% of its energy, routes a significant share through a chokepoint it cannot control, and runs a current account deficit driven by energy imports has a permanent security deficit. The $65/bbl assumption in Turkey's 2026 budget is now obsolete — every day of Hormuz disruption widens the fiscal gap and feeds inflation through the exchange-rate pass-through channel that makes Turkey uniquely vulnerable.

Iranian natural gas supplies of 15–25 million cubic meters per day are at risk. Turkey's total daily consumption is 150–170 million cubic meters. The Iran war doesn't just raise prices — it threatens a physical supply gap that no LNG diversification can fully close in the short term. The TurkeyInDepth Substack analysis puts the mechanism clearly: Hormuz is a multi-layered cost shock — intensifying inflationary pressures while straining external balances simultaneously, a double bind for an economy already running tight monetary policy at 37% interest rates.

The decisive move is to convert energy dependency into transit leverage. Turkey sits on potential alternative corridors — the Southern Gas Corridor, KRG pipeline revival, Eastern Mediterranean gas — but it has not moved fast enough to make itself indispensable as the alternative route. The trap: treating energy diversification as a cost-management problem rather than a geopolitical positioning exercise means Turkey remains a price-taker in every future crisis.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy import dependence is Turkey's deepest structural vulnerability — deeper than any single foreign policy dispute. Every energy crisis is a balance-of-payments crisis, an inflation crisis, and a political crisis simultaneously. Until Turkey controls its supply routes rather than merely shopping for them, it remains structurally exposed to any disruption in the Gulf.

sections.heading: The Lira Hold and the Growth Collapse
sections.body:
Chatham House economist Timothy Ash said on May 11 that the lira's resilience through the Iran war shock is a 'significant success' for Turkish economic management. The CBRT held the policy rate at 37%, suspended repo auctions, sold approximately $60 billion from reserves, and allowed funding rates to reach 40%. The lira has devalued only 5% year-to-date, trading at 45.17 to the dollar. FX reserves have recovered from $41.6 billion in March to $53.2 billion by late April, supplemented by $110 billion in gold.

The success is real — but it is a defensive success bought at a devastating structural cost. Annual inflation hit 32% in April. The manufacturing PMI collapsed to 46 in April, its lowest since September 2024. Exports fell 6% year-on-year in March to $22 billion; imports rose 8% to $33 billion, leaving an $11 billion monthly trade deficit. The IMF cut Turkey's growth forecast to 3.4%. Turkey is keeping the lira strong by making Turkish industry uncompetitive, imports cheap, and the current account deficit structural. This is not stability — it is an exchange-rate regime that is hollowing out the productive base to preserve the financial indicator.

Professor Emre Alkin at Topkapı University calls it straight: after three years, Turkey has become one of the most expensive countries in the world, deindustrialization is intensifying, and the cost of living has risen significantly. The strong-lira policy means Turkish exports are expensive and imports are attractive — exactly the wrong configuration for a country facing a permanent energy import bill. Economist Mustafa Sönmez notes that war-region export markets are contracting while energy import costs are rising — the worst possible combination for the current account.

Ash himself rejects devaluation as a solution, citing Turkey's high exchange-rate pass-through, and argues that no country fights inflation without sacrificing growth. But the sacrifice is now structural: the PMI at 46 means manufacturing contraction, not just slowdown. The decisive move is to separate financial stability from industrial policy — use macroprudential tools to protect the lira while targeting industrial support and export credit at the sectors that can survive a strong-currency environment. The trap: a lira that survives but an industrial base that doesn't is a Pyrrhic victory that locks Turkey into permanent current-account dependence.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's monetary policy is a bet that financial stability can outlast geopolitical disruption. But the industrial contraction data says the bet is being lost on the real economy even as it is won on the exchange rate. A country that preserves its currency while deindustrializing is trading strategic depth for financial optics.

sections.heading: The Kurdish Peace Process and Erdogan's Constitutional Gambit
sections.body:
The PKK peace process remains the single most consequential domestic variable shaping Turkey's foreign policy freedom of action. The imprisoned PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan called for a ceasefire in 2025; the group announced its dissolution in May 2025, with a symbolic disarmament in July. Turkey's parliament approved a peace roadmap in February 2026. But constitutional reform is required to advance the process — both to legalize PKK members' return and to devolve power to Kurdish municipalities. A senior Erdogan adviser confirmed in September 2025 that the constitution 'must change.'

The constitutional reform and the peace process are structurally fused — but not in the way the government presents. Erdogan needs pro-Kurdish party support in parliament to change the constitution to allow him to run for a third term in 2028. The Kurdish peace process is the vehicle for that support. This means the process is politically transactional, not institutionally irreversible — and every external shock that makes Kurdish cooperation more expensive for Erdogan (like the Iran war's effect on Kurdish calculations) makes the entire reform project fragile.

Foreign Affairs reports that a senior Turkish official confirmed 'the Kurds have made a strategic choice' not to enter the Iran war — specifically, the strongest Kurdish faction inside Iran (a PKK affiliate) declined U.S. and Israeli backing. This is good news for Ankara in the short term. But the same article warns that Trump briefly floated the idea of arming Iranian Kurdish forces before backing down. The Kurdish question can be reopened by any external power with the will to do so. The only reliable hedge is a domestic settlement that makes Kurdish cooperation with Turkey structurally preferable to any external alliance.

The decisive move is to make the peace process irreversible by passing legal reforms before the constitutional bargain — a law allowing PKK members to lay down arms and return, release of political prisoners, devolution of municipal powers. The trap: if Erdogan makes Kurdish reforms contingent on his own term-limit removal, the entire process becomes vulnerable to a single political failure. If the constitutional amendment fails, the peace process dies with it — and Turkey's most dangerous internal fault line reopens at exactly the moment the regional order is most volatile.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kurdish peace process is the precondition for every foreign policy play Turkey wants to make. Without it, any external actor can reopen Turkey's internal wound at will. With it, Turkey becomes structurally immune to the most exploitable vulnerability in its strategic profile. The fusion of that process with Erdogan's personal political survival is the single greatest risk to Turkish grand strategy.

sections.heading: Israel's Escalating Dominance and Turkey's Encirclement Anxiety
sections.body:
The Foreign Affairs analysis identifies Israel's expanding regional dominance as the structural threat most likely to outlast the Iran war itself. Israel has struck Syrian air bases Turkey was considering for use. It has deepened defense cooperation with Greece and Cyprus explicitly framed as counter-Turkey. Israeli commentators increasingly cast Turkey as a long-term threat. Turkish officials fear a postwar order where Israel, emboldened by a successful Iran campaign, consolidates military presence in Syria and Lebanon while Turkey finds itself with less leverage in the Eastern Mediterranean and fewer options for a Washington reset.

The structural reality is that Israel is winning the regional power competition while Turkey is sitting out. Israel's operational demonstration in Iran — sustained strikes, missile defense effectiveness, intelligence penetration — has reshaped the regional perception of military power. Turkey's neutrality preserves the option of future engagement with all parties, but it also preserves the current imbalance where Israel acts and Turkey watches. A regional order built on Israeli military primacy and Turkish diplomatic neutrality is one where Turkey is structurally contained.

The technical deconfliction channel in Syria, brokered by the U.S., is operationally useful but strategically insignificant. It manages accidents; it does not manage competition. The DefenseNews reporting confirms that Turkey's defense minister is seeking an Article 5 reaffirmation at the Ankara NATO summit this summer — which is a signal that Ankara's primary hedge against Israeli (or any) pressure remains the NATO umbrella, not bilateral capability or regional alliances.

The decisive move is to build independent deterrence credibility — the Yildirimhan is a start in one domain, but Turkey needs anti-access/area-denial capability in the Eastern Mediterranean (naval, drone, and electronic warfare) that makes Israeli operational planning factor in Turkish costs. The trap: relying on NATO Article 5 as the sole deterrent against a non-NATO rival (Israel) is a miscalculation — Article 5 covers attacks by states, not strategic competition or encirclement through third-country basing and alliance-building.
sections.whyItMatters: Israel's regional position is being hardened by the Iran war. Turkey's is not. The gap between Israeli operational reach and Turkish defensive depth widens with each week of the conflict. If the postwar order consolidates around Israeli preeminence, Turkey's Eastern Mediterranean position, its Syria leverage, and its NATO renegotiation posture all erode simultaneously.

bottomLine: Turkey is holding the line — lira stable, mediation active, ICBM unveiled, NATO command secured. But holding the line is not the same as advancing the position. The Iran war is converting Turkey's temporary indispensability — its geography, its radar, its hosting — into permanent exposure faster than Ankara is converting it into permanent institutional power. The PESCO door stays shut. The air defense gap stays open. The energy import bill stays structural. The Kurdish peace process stays fused to one man's political survival. The lira survives by sacrificing industry. Israel's dominance consolidates while Turkey watches. Every one of these is a structural deficit that tactical competence cannot fix. The window to convert crisis indispensability into postwar institutional power is measured in months, not years — and it is closing while Turkey is still performing neutrality.
sources: DefenseNews (Yildirimhan propellant, Turkey EU defense role), Foreign Affairs (Iran war's threat to Turkey), Stimson Center (Turkey mediation in Iran war), Carnegie Europe (Europe-Turkey relationship), SWP Berlin (Turkey in European security architecture), TurkeyToday (Yildirimhan ICBM specs, Timothy Ash lira analysis), AGBI (monetary policy, PMI, inflation data), World Bank (Hormuz oil shock), UNCTAD (Hormuz trade disruption), TEPAV (Hormuz impact on Turkey), S&P Global (Turkish inflation outlook), Reuters (Turkey-Iran mediation, PKK peace roadmap), Trading Economics (Turkey interest rate), TurkishMinute (CBRT inflation forecast), JNS (Yildirimhan ICBM), Israel Hayom (SAHA 2026), International Defence Analysis (Yildirimhan), Anadolu Agency (SAHA 2026 photos)
